title: Incarcerated Inguinal Hernia in Infant
chief_complaint: My baby has a bulge in his right groin that won't go away. He's been crying inconsolably for 3 hours and vomiting. The area is getting red and he screams when I touch it.

physical_exam.Genitalia: Right inguinal region: Firm, tender, erythematous mass extending from inguinal canal into scrotum. Non-reducible. No transillumination (differentiates from hydrocele - which transilluminates). Left side: Normal testis, small reducible bulge (bilateral hernias).
physical_exam.Abdomen: Distended. Hyperactive bowel sounds. No peritoneal signs currently.

hidden_data.inguinal_us: Right inguinal hernia containing bowel loops with peristalsis present. Normal vascular flow on Doppler (viable bowel - not strangulated yet). No free fluid. Right testis with normal flow.
hidden_data.abdominal_xr: Dilated small bowel loops. Air in hernia sac. No free air.
hidden_data.cmp: Normal electrolytes. Lactate 1.5 (not elevated - bowel still viable).
hidden_data.reduction: Attempted reduction in ED with sedation: Unsuccessful after 2 attempts.

correct_diagnosis: Incarcerated Right Inguinal Hernia (Non-Reducible) in Infant - URGENT SURGICAL EXPLORATION
key_learning_points: Inguinal hernia = most common surgical condition in infants. Incidence 1-5% of newborns (30% in premature infants). Male:Female 6:1. Right side 60%, Left 30%, Bilateral 10%

Pathophysiology: Patent processus vaginalis (failure of obliteration after testicular descent) → abdominal contents (bowel, omentum, ovary) herniate through internal ring into inguinal canal. Prematurity = highest risk (patent processus vaginalis in 80% of preterms <30 weeks)

Incarcerated vs Strangulated: Incarcerated = non-reducible but viable (warm, normal color, no peritonitis). Strangulated = incarcerated + vascular compromise (tender, erythematous, edematous, systemic toxicity, peritonitis). Incarceration risk highest in first 6 months (30% in infants <6 months vs <5% in older children)

Management of incarcerated hernia: Attempt reduction (Trendelenburg position, sedation/analgesia, ice pack to reduce swelling, gentle sustained pressure). If successful: Admit 24h observation, elective herniotomy in 24-48h (after edema subsides). If unsuccessful or signs of strangulation: EMERGENCY SURGERY

Surgery: High ligation of hernia sac at internal ring (herniotomy). Do NOT perform herniorrhaphy (tissue repair) in infants - not needed, may damage cord structures. Laparoscopic repair increasingly used. ALWAYS explore contralateral side in infants <1 year (bilateral in 60%) or if contralateral patent processus vaginalis on laparoscopy. Ovaries in girls may be sliding component (not true hernia) - preserve ovary unless necrotic

Complications: Strangulated bowel → necrosis → perforation → peritonitis → sepsis. Testicular atrophy from compression of testicular vessels. Recurrence <1%. Premature infants often repaired before NICU discharge due to high incarceration risk
